title: Fast Synthesis of Basic Oscillators
excerpt: I’ve been working on synthesizers for the GBA and rp2040 the past couple years, and I’ve made some fast oscillators.
content_html:
2026-07-23

I’ve been working on synthesizers for the GBA and rp2040 the past couple years, and I’ve made some fast oscillators.

So first of all, this all applies to doing synthesis with integers. I think for oscillators, even if you do have an FPU, ints are much easier to deal with anyway because of the wrapping.

We’ll be doing math entirely with 32-bit values in this post, for two reasons. One is, it’s the native register size on the GBA, and what I’ve been doing with for the past couple years. The other: it’s a very nice sweet spot for pitch resolution. You can represent all pitches within the audible range quite accurately when dealing with a 32-bit phase. As you reduce the bit-depth of your phase, you start losing pitch accuracy in the bass. You can work with that, and many demosceners do, but it becomes something you have to actively think about when composing your music. With 32-bit, its a non-issue. Anything beyond 32-bit doesn’t really provide much benefit.

Most of the waveforms I’m showing here will have aliasing problems. The sharp transitions create audible artifacts that don’t sound super great. One way to fix this is with PolyBLEP.

I’ve never implemented that, and anyway the point of this code is to be fast at all cost. This is for super constrained environments, where you have to accept the aliasing is there and move on. For alias-free sounds on a budget, you can use phase modulation between sine waves and you’ll get super clean sounds for very cheap, though not as cheap as these waves. I’ll do another post about that. If I ever do a fast PolyBLEP I’ll post about that too, but it’s not on my to-do right now.

TOC

  • Saw
  • Wave from Memory
  • Square
  • Triangle
  • Pulse Waves
  • Parabola approximation of a sine wave

Saw

All oscillators start life as a saw wave, and a saw wave has two components: the current phase, and a value added to the phase each audio sample.

phase: u32 = 0
step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32
outbuf: [i32; num_samples]

Each audio sample, we add the step to our phase, and the output of our oscillator is the phase. This gives us a ramp-up saw.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// str phase, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1     /|        /|
     / |       / |
    /  |      /  |
   /   |     /   |
0 /    |    /    |  
       |   /     |   /
       |  /      |  /
       | /       | /
-1     |/        |/

Each time phase overflows, we’ve completed one oscillation.

If you’re familiar with the concept of the nyquist frequency, it’s quite obvious here: when our phase step is 0x80000000, our wave oscillates once every two-samples, at exactly 1/2 our sample rate. If our phase step is higher than 0x80000000, we start overflowing more than once every two samples, but our output actually starts slowly rolling backwards. This also flips our saw wave to a ramp-down saw instead of a ramp-up. Taken as a signed integer, phases >= 0x80000000 are negative. Naturally, as our phase step grows beyond 0x80000000 we start oscillating slower and slower.

Wave from memory

If you shift down your saw, you can use it to index a wave stored in memory, and do all the wave table synthesis your heart desires. You’ll get the best performance with 8-bit waves, because it takes an extra instruction to mask off the low address bits when accessing 16 or 32-bit waves. So I usually use 8-bit waves, as I don’t find waveforms benefit from the dynamic range of 16-bits the way samples do.

This example uses 1024-sample waves, adjust the bit shifts for other sizes. Make sure to store your wave somewhere with fast access times.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

let wavedata: [i8; 1024] = /* ... */

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	let out = wavedata[(phase >> 22) as usize];

	// Scale up the output if you need to, or
	// leave it 8-bit to save some cycles cycles
	// going into a fixed-point volume control :)
	outbuf[i] = out;
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// lsr out, phase, 22
// ldrsb out, [wavedata, out]
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

Square

There’s two approaches you can take depending on personal taste, and what’s fast for your hardware.

Mathematic calculation

Calculating mathematically is best if you don’t have branchless conditionals. This is true of thumbv1, like you find on common Cortex-M0+ processors. It’s fine everywhere else too, so reach for this first.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// out = 0x00000000 when phase  < 0x80000000
	// out = 0xFFFFFFFF when phase >= 0x80000000
	let mut out = (phase as i32) >> 31;

	// out = INT_MIN when phase  < 0x80000000
	// out = INT_MAX when phase >= 0x80000000
	out = out + (-0x80000000);

	outbuf[i] = out;
}

// arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// asr out, phase, 31    /* can be single step with rsb
// sub out, 0x80000000      if 0x80000000 is put in register */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

// thumb assembly (cannot subtract large immediates like arm32)
// movs dc_offset, 0x80
// lsls dc_offset, 24
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// asrs out, phase, 31
// subs out, dc_offset
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1          _________        _________
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
0          |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
-1 ________|       |________|       |

For thumb, you can sacrifice a bit of precision and an extra cycle for each iteration to get rid of the dc offset register:

// loop:
// adds phase, step
// asrs out, phase, 31
// subs out, 0x80
// lsls out, 24
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

Conditional calculation

This second algorithm is only fast if you have branchless-conditionals. arm32 can make any instruction conditional, and thumbv2 has the IT instruction to form a small branchless conditional block, so this algorithm is good on both of those, and it’s what I use for the GBA. The main advantage over the mathematic calculation is it’s perfectly centered around 0, and it’s easy to control the amplitude of the square coming out of it by adjusting the constants. It may or may not fit better into the signal chain around it depending on what you’ve got going on.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// We use 0x7F000000 instead of 0x7FFFFFFF,
	// because it takes fewer cyles to load a
	// constant with <= 8 sigfigs on arm/thumb
	if ((phase & 0x80000000) == 0) {
		outbuf[i] = -0x7F000000;
	else {
		outbuf[i] = 0x7F000000;
	}

	outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
}

// Arm assembly. thumbv2 would be similar, but use `IT`
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// movpl out, -0x7F000000
// movmi out, 0x7F000000
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

Triangle

To explain the theory here I’m going to lead with an implementation using conditionals, but then I’ll give you a conditional-free variant that works the same way but is a little less clear as to what’s going on.

Uni-polar triangle wave with Conditionals

A triangle wave ramps up and then back down again. This is rather annoying to express in floating point, but here in integer land it’s quite easy:

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	if (phase & 0x80000000 == 0) {
		outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
	} else {
		outbuf[i] = (!phase) as i32;
	}
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// movpl out, phase  /* count-up in +phase */
// mvnmi out, phase  /* count-down in -phase */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1     -       -
     / \     / \
    /   \   /   \
   /     \ /     \
0 -       -       -



-1

Note that ! is the one’s complement operator, meaning all the bits in phase get flipped.

This ramps up normally from 0 to 0x7FFFFFFF. Once we hit 0x80000000, we start inverting the number. !0x80000000 = 0x7FFFFFFF, !0x80000001 = 0x7FFFFFFE, and so on, counting all the back down to !0xFFFFFFFF = 0.

Note that this means our triangle has very slightly flat tips, since 0 appears twice, and so does 0x7FFFFFFF. When dealing with 32-bit phase, you will never actually hear this artifact, but it might become more apparent with smaller phases.

Taken as a signed integer, !x = (-x) - 1. You might wonder, why don’t we use the actual - operator to take the twos complement, or use the .abs() absolute value function? It seems like it would get rid of our flat tips! Well, it almost does, except there is a big problem: INT_MIN.abs() == INT_MIN. The twos-complement of 0x80000000 is 0x80000000. When we output a signed audio sample, our wave would have a very nasty wrap-around negative spike at the tip, like this:

1      |        |
      /|\      /|
     / | \    / |
    /  |  \  /  |
0  /   |   \/   |
       |        |
       |        |
       |        |
       |        |
-1     |        |

Bi-polar triangle wave with Conditionals

Outputting a uni-polar wave can be fine sometimes, but usually you want a wave that oscillates between negative and positive. Our solution to that is crude. We just subtract the DC-offset from our triangle, and there you have it.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	if (phase & 0x80000000 == 0) {
		outbuf[i] = (phase as i32) - 0x40000000;
	} else {
		outbuf[i] = ((!phase) as i32) - 0x40000000;
	}
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// movpl out, phase
// mvnmi out, phase
// sub out, 0x40000000
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1          

      -       -
     / \     / \
0   /   \   /   \
   /     \ /     \
  -       -       -

-1

This produces a triangle wave which does not span the full i32 range, instead spanning from -0.5 to +0.5. That’s never an issue in practice, since you’re always going into some sort of volume control after generating the oscillator, and you can account for the loss in volume somewhere in that signal chain.

Mathematic calculation of the triangle

The conditional implementation is easy to understand, but you can do the exact same thing mathematically, in fewer instructions.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// Will be all-zeroes if phase is positive or all-ones if phase is negative
	let mut out = (phase as i32) >> 31;

	// When phase is positive, out = phase
	// When phase is negative, out = !phase
	out = out ^ (phase as i32);

	// Remove DC-offset
	outbuf[i] = out - 0x40000000;
}

// arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// eor out, phase, phase, asr 31
// sub out, 0x40000000
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

Pulse Waves

25%, 12.5%, 6.25%, etc.

By AND-ing the top bits before the square generation step, we can further limit our square wave’s duty cycle to get a pulse wave.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	let mut out = phase;

	// Comment out lines starting from the bottom
	// to achieve desired pulse width.
	out = out & (phase << 1); // Cut pulse width in half to 25%
	out = out & (phase << 2); // Cut again to 12.5%
	// out = out & (phase << 3); // Cut again to 6.25%

	let mut out = (out as i32) >> 31;

	// out = INT_MIN when phase  < 0x80000000
	// out = INT_MAX when phase >= 0x80000000
	out = out + (-0x80000000);

	outbuf[i] = out;
}

// arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// and out, phase, phase, lsl 1
// and out, out, phase, lsl 2
// asr out, 31           /* can be single step with rsb
// sub out, 0x80000000      if 0x80000000 is put in register */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1              _____            _____
               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
0              |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
-1 ____________|   |____________|   |

16.6%, 25%, 33.3%, 40%

We can also generate pulse waves by phase-modulating a square wave carrier with a uni-polar triangle wave modulator. By changing the amplitude of the triangle, we can pick a few pulse widths the previous method can’t provide.

While the previous method elongates the low edge, this method elongates the high edge.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	let mut tri = phase as i32;
	tri = tri ^ (tri >> 31);

	// Choose your fighter
	//
	// 1/6 wave
	// tri = tri << 1
	//
	// 1/4 wave
	// tri = tri >> 0
	//
	// 1/3 wave
	// tri = tri >> 1
	//
	// 2/5 wave
	// tri = tri >> 2
	//
	// square wave (no modulation)
	// tri = 0;

	let square_phase = phase.wrapping_add(tri as u32);
	let mut square = (square_phase as i32) >> 31;
	square -= 0x80000000;
	
	outbuf[i] = square;
}

// Arm assembly
// mov dc_offset, 0x80000000;
// loop:
// add phase, step
// eor out, phase, phase, asr 31  /* out = tri */
// add out, phase, out, lsl 1     /* out = sq phase, shift sets width */
// asr out, phase, 31    /* can be single step with rsb
// sub out, 0x80000000      if 0x80000000 is put in register */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1      _____________    _____________
       |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
0      |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
-1 ____|           |____|           |

Adding a phase-offset to the triangle does further interesting things to the pulses, but that’s outside the scope of this post.

Parabola approximation of a sine wave

I’m stretching the limits of “basic” here, but it’s an approximation of a basic wave so we’ll do it for fun.

If you have a fast 32-bit multiply instruction, you can get an approximation of a sine wave by taking a parabola for the first half of the phase, and then the negation of that parabola for the second half of the phase.

This approximation is nowhere close to accurate enough for physics or graphics, but for audio it will get you a sound that is closer to a pure tone than a triangle wave, without being as pure as a proper sine.

The usecase for this is very niche. On the GBA, I don’t do this, because it’s slower than just accessing a sine lookup table in ROM. On the rp2040 I don’t do this, because while ROM access is slow, there’s plenty of SRAM to keep a sine table in. On a high end system, there’s so much cache that you can pre-load a sine table into cache if you’re chasing perf instead of doing this.

Best I can tell the only real reason to use this is if you’re doing some size coding and don’t have space for a lookup table, or you want the unique sound of it.

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// Saw wave at 2x frequency
	let mut out: i32 = (phase << 1) as i32;

	// Saw -> Unipolar triangle (see Triangle section)
	// Range 0 - 0x7FFFFFFF
	let mut out = out ^ (out >> 31);

	// We are going to calculate X - X^2 to get a
	// parabolic arc from 0 to half-phase. This is
	// can also be thought of as
	// lerp(from: X, to: 0, alpha: X)
	let x = out;

	// Scale to 0 - 0x7FFF
	let mut out = phase >> 16;

	// Output arc from 0 - 0x4000FFFE
	out = out * out;
	out = x - out

	// Negate the second arc in the second half of
	// the phase
	out = out ^ ((phase as i32) >> 31);

	// Final output: -0x4000FFFF - 0x4000FFFE
	outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// lsl out, phase, 1
// eor out, out, out, asr 31
// mov x, out
// lsr out, 16
// mov tmp, out    /* arm limitation: cant square
// mul out, tmp       with single reg */
// sub out, x, out
// eor out, out, phase, asr 31
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

Once we hit 0x80000000, we start inverting the number. !0x80000000 = 0x7FFFFFFF, !0x80000001 = 0x7FFFFFFE, and so on, counting all the back down to !0xFFFFFFFF = 0. Note that this means our triangle has very slightly flat tips, since 0 appears twice, and so does 0x7FFFFFFF. When dealing with 32-bit phase, you will never actually hear this artifact, but it might become more apparent with smaller phases. Taken as a signed integer, !x = (-x) - 1. You might wonder, why don’t we use the actual - operator to take the twos complement, or use the .abs() absolute value function? It seems like it would get rid of our flat tips! Well, it almost does, except there is a big problem: INT_MIN.abs() == INT_MIN. The twos-complement of 0x80000000 is 0x80000000. When we output a signed audio sample, our wave would have a very nasty wrap-around negative spike at the tip, like this: 1 | | /|\ /| / | \ / | / | \ / | 0 / | \/ | | | | | | | | | -1 | | Bi-polar triangle wave with Conditionals Outputting a uni-polar wave can be fine sometimes, but usually you want a wave that oscillates between negative and positive. Our solution to that is crude. We just subtract the DC-offset from our triangle, and there you have it. let mut phase: u32 = 0; let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32; let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples]; for i in 0..num_samples { phase = phase.wrapping_add(step); if (phase & 0x80000000 == 0) { outbuf[i] = (phase as i32) - 0x40000000; } else { outbuf[i] = ((!phase) as i32) - 0x40000000; } } // Arm assembly // loop: // adds phase, step // movpl out, phase // mvnmi out, phase // sub out, 0x40000000 // str out, [outbuf], 4 // subs count, 1 // bne loop 1 - - / \ / \ 0 / \ / \ / \ / \ - - - -1 This produces a triangle wave which does not span the full i32 range, instead spanning from -0.5 to +0.5. That’s never an issue in practice, since you’re always going into some sort of volume control after generating the oscillator, and you can account for the loss in volume somewhere in that signal chain. Mathematic calculation of the triangle The conditional implementation is easy to understand, but you can do the exact same thing mathematically, in fewer instructions. let mut phase: u32 = 0; let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32; let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples]; for i in 0..num_samples { phase = phase.wrapping_add(step); // Will be all-zeroes if phase is positive or all-ones if phase is negative let mut out = (phase as i32) >> 31; // When phase is positive, out = phase // When phase is negative, out = !phase out = out ^ (phase as i32); // Remove DC-offset outbuf[i] = out - 0x40000000; } // arm assembly // loop: // add phase, step // eor out, phase, phase, asr 31 // sub out, 0x40000000 // str out, [outbuf], 4 // subs count, 1 // bne loop Pulse Waves 25%, 12.5%, 6.25%, etc. By AND-ing the top bits before the square generation step, we can further limit our square wave’s duty cycle to get a pulse wave. let mut phase: u32 = 0; let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32; let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples]; for i in 0..num_samples { phase = phase.wrapping_add(step); let mut out = phase; // Comment out lines starting from the bottom // to achieve desired pulse width. out = out & (phase << 1); // Cut pulse width in half to 25% out = out & (phase << 2); // Cut again to 12.5% // out = out & (phase << 3); // Cut again to 6.25% let mut out = (out as i32) >> 31; // out = INT_MIN when phase < 0x80000000 // out = INT_MAX when phase >= 0x80000000 out = out + (-0x80000000); outbuf[i] = out; } // arm assembly // loop: // add phase, step // and out, phase, phase, lsl 1 // and out, out, phase, lsl 2 // asr out, 31 /* can be single step with rsb // sub out, 0x80000000 if 0x80000000 is put in register */ // str out, [outbuf], 4 // subs count, 1 // bne loop 1 _____ _____ | | | | | | | | | | | | 0 | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | -1 ____________| |____________| | 16.6%, 25%, 33.3%, 40% We can also generate pulse waves by phase-modulating a square wave carrier with a uni-polar triangle wave modulator. By changing the amplitude of the triangle, we can pick a few pulse widths the previous method can’t provide. While the previous method elongates the low edge, this method elongates the high edge. let mut phase: u32 = 0; let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32; let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples]; for i in 0..num_samples { phase = phase.wrapping_add(step); let mut tri = phase as i32; tri = tri ^ (tri >> 31); // Choose your fighter // // 1/6 wave // tri = tri << 1 // // 1/4 wave // tri = tri >> 0 // // 1/3 wave // tri = tri >> 1 // // 2/5 wave // tri = tri >> 2 // // square wave (no modulation) // tri = 0; let square_phase = phase.wrapping_add(tri as u32); let mut square = (square_phase as i32) >> 31; square -= 0x80000000; outbuf[i] = square; } // Arm assembly // mov dc_offset, 0x80000000; // loop: // add phase, step // eor out, phase, phase, asr 31 /* out = tri */ // add out, phase, out, lsl 1 /* out = sq phase, shift sets width */ // asr out, phase, 31 /* can be single step with rsb // sub out, 0x80000000 if 0x80000000 is put in register */ // str out, [outbuf], 4 // subs count, 1 // bne loop 1 _____________ _____________ | | | | | | | | | | | | 0 | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | -1 ____| |____| | Adding a phase-offset to the triangle does further interesting things to the pulses, but that’s outside the scope of this post. Parabola approximation of a sine wave I’m stretching the limits of “basic” here, but it’s an approximation of a basic wave so we’ll do it for fun. If you have a fast 32-bit multiply instruction, you can get an approximation of a sine wave by taking a parabola for the first half of the phase, and then the negation of that parabola for the second half of the phase. This approximation is nowhere close to accurate enough for physics or graphics, but for audio it will get you a sound that is closer to a pure tone than a triangle wave, without being as pure as a proper sine. The usecase for this is very niche. On the GBA, I don’t do this, because it’s slower than just accessing a sine lookup table in ROM. On the rp2040 I don’t do this, because while ROM access is slow, there’s plenty of SRAM to keep a sine table in. On a high end system, there’s so much cache that you can pre-load a sine table into cache if you’re chasing perf instead of doing this. Best I can tell the only real reason to use this is if you’re doing some size coding and don’t have space for a lookup table, or you want the unique sound of it. let mut phase: u32 = 0; let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32; let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples]; for i in 0..num_samples { phase = phase.wrapping_add(step); // Saw wave at 2x frequency let mut out: i32 = (phase << 1) as i32; // Saw -> Unipolar triangle (see Triangle section) // Range 0 - 0x7FFFFFFF let mut out = out ^ (out >> 31); // We are going to calculate X - X^2 to get a // parabolic arc from 0 to half-phase. This is // can also be thought of as // lerp(from: X, to: 0, alpha: X) let x = out; // Scale to 0 - 0x7FFF let mut out = phase >> 16; // Output arc from 0 - 0x4000FFFE out = out * out; out = x - out // Negate the second arc in the second half of // the phase out = out ^ ((phase as i32) >> 31); // Final output: -0x4000FFFF - 0x4000FFFE outbuf[i] = phase as i32; } // Arm assembly // loop: // add phase, step // lsl out, phase, 1 // eor out, out, out, asr 31 // mov x, out // lsr out, 16 // mov tmp, out /* arm limitation: cant square // mul out, tmp with single reg */ // sub out, x, out // eor out, out, phase, asr 31 // str out, [outbuf], 4 // subs count, 1 // bne loop It’s hard to do this one justice with ascii-art, so here’s a proper render:

blocks.html_en: I’ve been working on synthesizers for the GBA and rp2040 the past couple years, and I’ve made some fast oscillators.

sentences.en: I’ve been working on synthesizers for the GBA and rp2040 the past couple years, and I’ve made some fast oscillators.
sentences.zh: 过去几年我一直致力于 GBA 和 rp2040 的合成器，并且制作了一些快速振荡器。

blocks.html_en: So first of all, this all applies to doing synthesis with integers. I think for oscillators, even if you do have an FPU, ints are much easier to deal with anyway because of the wrapping.

sentences.en: So first of all, this all applies to doing synthesis with integers.
sentences.zh: 首先，这一切都适用于整数综合。

sentences.en: I think for oscillators, even if you do have an FPU, ints are much easier to deal with anyway because of the wrapping.
sentences.zh: 我认为对于振荡器，即使您确实有 FPU，由于包装，整数也更容易处理。

blocks.html_en: We’ll be doing math entirely with 32-bit values in this post, for two reasons. One is, it’s the native register size on the GBA, and what I’ve been doing with for the past couple years. The other: it’s a very nice sweet spot for pitch resolution. You can represent all pitches within the audible range quite accurately when dealing with a 32-bit phase. As you reduce the bit-depth of your phase, you start losing pitch accuracy in the bass. You can work with that, and many demosceners do, but it becomes something you have to actively think about when composing your music. With 32-bit, its a non-issue. Anything beyond 32-bit doesn’t really provide much benefit.

sentences.en: The other: it’s a very nice sweet spot for pitch resolution.
sentences.zh: 另一个：这是音高分辨率的一个非常好的最佳位置。

sentences.zh: 处理 32 位相位时，您可以非常准确地表示可听范围内的所有音高。

sentences.en: As you reduce the bit-depth of your phase, you start losing pitch accuracy in the bass.
sentences.zh: 当您减少相位的位深度时，您开始失去低音的音高准确性。

sentences.en: You can work with that, and many demosceners do, but it becomes something you have to actively think about when composing your music.
sentences.zh: 你可以使用它，许多演示者也这样做，但这成为你在创作音乐时必须积极思考的事情。

blocks.html_en: Most of the waveforms I’m showing here will have aliasing problems. The sharp transitions create audible artifacts that don’t sound super great. One way to fix this is with PolyBLEP.

This is for super constrained environments, where you have to accept the aliasing is there and move on. For alias-free sounds on a budget, you can use phase modulation between sine waves and you’ll get super clean sounds for very cheap, though not as cheap as these waves. I’ll do another post about that. If I ever do a fast PolyBLEP I’ll post about that too, but it’s not on my to-do right now.

blocks.html_en: Saw
All oscillators start life as a saw wave, and a saw wave has two components: the current phase, and a value added to the phase each audio sample.

sentences.en: All oscillators start life as a saw wave, and a saw wave has two components: the current phase, and a value added to the phase each audio sample.
sentences.zh: 所有振荡器的生命周期都是锯齿波，锯齿波有两个组成部分：当前相位以及添加到每个音频样本相位的值。

blocks.html_en: Each audio sample, we add the step to our phase, and the output of our oscillator is the phase. This gives us a ramp-up saw.

sentences.en: Each audio sample, we add the step to our phase, and the output of our oscillator is the phase.
sentences.zh: 每个音频样本，我们将步长添加到相位中，振荡器的输出就是相位。

sentences.en: This gives us a ramp-up saw.
sentences.zh: 这给了我们一个斜坡锯。

blocks.html_en: Each time phase overflows, we’ve completed one oscillation.

sentences.en: Each time phase overflows, we’ve completed one oscillation.
sentences.zh: 每次相位溢出，我们就完成了一次振荡。

blocks.html_en: If you’re familiar with the concept of the nyquist frequency, it’s quite obvious here: when our phase step is 0x80000000, our wave oscillates once every two-samples, at exactly 1/2 our sample rate. If our phase step is higher than 0x80000000, we start overflowing more than once every two samples, but our output actually starts slowly rolling backwards. This also flips our saw wave to a ramp-down saw instead of a ramp-up. Taken as a signed integer, phases >= 0x80000000 are negative. Naturally, as our phase step grows beyond 0x80000000 we start oscillating slower and slower.

sentences.en: If you’re familiar with the concept of the nyquist frequency, it’s quite obvious here: when our phase step is 0x80000000, our wave oscillates once every two-samples, at exactly 1/2 our sample rate.
sentences.zh: 如果您熟悉奈奎斯特频率的概念，那么这里就很明显了：当我们的相位步长为 0x80000000 时，我们的波每两个样本振荡一次，恰好是我们采样率的 1/2。

sentences.en: If our phase step is higher than 0x80000000, we start overflowing more than once every two samples, but our output actually starts slowly rolling backwards.
sentences.zh: 如果我们的相位步长高于 0x80000000，我们就会开始每两个样本溢出一次以上，但我们的输出实际上开始缓慢向后滚动。

sentences.en: This also flips our saw wave to a ramp-down saw instead of a ramp-up.
sentences.zh: 这也会将我们的锯波翻转为斜坡下降锯波而不是斜坡上升。

sentences.en: Taken as a signed integer, phases >= 0x80000000 are negative.
sentences.zh: 作为有符号整数，相位 >= 0x80000000 为负数。

sentences.en: Naturally, as our phase step grows beyond 0x80000000 we start oscillating slower and slower.
sentences.zh: 自然地，当我们的相位步长超过 0x80000000 时，我们开始振荡得越来越慢。

blocks.html_en: If you shift down your saw, you can use it to index a wave stored in memory, and do all the wave table synthesis your heart desires. You’ll get the best performance with 8-bit waves, because it takes an extra instruction to mask off the low address bits when accessing 16 or 32-bit waves. So I usually use 8-bit waves, as I don’t find waveforms benefit from the dynamic range of 16-bits the way samples do.

content_html_zh:
2026-07-23

过去几年我一直致力于 GBA 和 rp2040 的合成器，并且制作了一些快速振荡器。

首先，这一切都适用于整数综合。 我认为对于振荡器，即使您确实有 FPU，由于包装，整数也更容易处理。

在这篇文章中，我们将完全使用 32 位值进行数学计算，原因有两个。 一是，它是 GBA 上的本机寄存器大小，也是我过去几年一直在做的事情。 另一个：这是音高分辨率的一个非常好的最佳位置。 处理 32 位相位时，您可以非常准确地表示可听范围内的所有音高。 当您减少相位的位深度时，您开始失去低音的音高准确性。 你可以使用它，许多演示者也这样做，但这成为你在创作音乐时必须积极思考的事情。 对于 32 位，这不是问题。 任何超过 32 位的东西都不会真正提供太多好处。

我在这里显示的大多数波形都会出现混叠问题。 尖锐的过渡会产生声音伪影，听起来不太好。 解决此问题的一种方法是使用 PolyBLEP。

我从未实现过这一点，无论如何，这段代码的目的是不惜一切代价保持快速。 这是针对超级受限的环境，您必须接受别名的存在并继续前进。 对于预算内的无混叠声音，您可以在正弦波之间使用相位调制，并且您将以非常便宜的价格获得超级干净的声音，尽管不像这些波那么便宜。 我将就此发表另一篇文章。 如果我做过快速 PolyBLEP，我也会发布相关内容，但这不是我现在的待办事项。

Saw

所有振荡器的生命周期都是锯齿波，锯齿波有两个组成部分：当前相位以及添加到每个音频样本相位的值。

phase: u32 = 0
step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32
outbuf: [i32; num_samples]

每个音频样本，我们将步长添加到相位中，振荡器的输出就是相位。 这给了我们一个斜坡锯。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// str phase, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1     /|        /|
     / |       / |
    /  |      /  |
   /   |     /   |
0 /    |    /    |  
       |   /     |   /
       |  /      |  /
       | /       | /
-1     |/        |/

每次相位溢出，我们就完成了一次振荡。

如果您熟悉奈奎斯特频率的概念，那么这里就很明显了：当我们的相位步长为 0x80000000 时，我们的波每两个样本振荡一次，恰好是我们采样率的 1/2。 如果我们的相位步长高于 0x80000000，我们就会开始每两个样本溢出一次以上，但我们的输出实际上开始缓慢向后滚动。 这也会将我们的锯波翻转为斜坡下降锯波而不是斜坡上升。 作为有符号整数，相位 >= 0x80000000 为负数。 自然地，当我们的相位步长超过 0x80000000 时，我们开始振荡得越来越慢。

记忆中的波浪

如果你向下移动锯子，你可以用它来索引存储在内存中的波，并进行你想要的所有波表合成。 使用 8 位波形可以获得最佳性能，因为在访问 16 或 32 位波形时需要额外的指令来屏蔽低地址位。 因此，我通常使用 8 位波形，因为我发现波形不像样本那样受益于 16 位的动态范围。

本示例使用 1024 个样本波形，调整其他大小的位移位。 确保将您的波形存储在访问速度快的地方。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

let wavedata: [i8; 1024] = /* ... */

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	let out = wavedata[(phase >> 22) as usize];

	// Scale up the output if you need to, or
	// leave it 8-bit to save some cycles cycles
	// going into a fixed-point volume control :)
	outbuf[i] = out;
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// lsr out, phase, 22
// ldrsb out, [wavedata, out]
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

正方形

您可以采取两种方法，具体取决于个人喜好以及对您的硬件来说速度最快的方法。

数学计算

如果没有无分支条件，最好进行数学计算。 对于thumbv1 来说也是如此，就像您在常见的Cortex-M0+ 处理器上看到的那样。 其他地方也很好，所以先接触这个。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// out = 0x00000000 when phase  < 0x80000000
	// out = 0xFFFFFFFF when phase >= 0x80000000
	let mut out = (phase as i32) >> 31;

	// out = INT_MIN when phase  < 0x80000000
	// out = INT_MAX when phase >= 0x80000000
	out = out + (-0x80000000);

	outbuf[i] = out;
}

// arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// asr out, phase, 31    /* can be single step with rsb
// sub out, 0x80000000      if 0x80000000 is put in register */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

// thumb assembly (cannot subtract large immediates like arm32)
// movs dc_offset, 0x80
// lsls dc_offset, 24
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// asrs out, phase, 31
// subs out, dc_offset
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1          _________        _________
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
0          |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
           |       |        |       |
-1 ________|       |________|       |

对于拇指，您可以牺牲一点精度和每次迭代的额外周期来摆脱直流偏移寄存器：

// loop:
// adds phase, step
// asrs out, phase, 31
// subs out, 0x80
// lsls out, 24
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

条件计算

仅当您有无分支条件时，第二种算法才会很快。 arm32可以使任何指令成为条件，而thumbv2有IT指令来形成一个小的无分支条件块，所以这个算法在这两个方面都很好，这就是我在GBA中使用的。 相对于数学计算的主要优点是它完全以 0 为中心，并且可以通过调整常数轻松控制从中得出的平方的幅度。 它可能更适合也可能不适合周围的信号链，具体取决于您正在做什么。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// We use 0x7F000000 instead of 0x7FFFFFFF,
	// because it takes fewer cyles to load a
	// constant with <= 8 sigfigs on arm/thumb
	if ((phase & 0x80000000) == 0) {
		outbuf[i] = -0x7F000000;
	else {
		outbuf[i] = 0x7F000000;
	}

	outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
}

// Arm assembly. thumbv2 would be similar, but use `IT`
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// movpl out, -0x7F000000
// movmi out, 0x7F000000
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

三角形

为了解释这里的理论，我将首先使用条件来实现，然后我将给你一个无条件的变体，它的工作方式相同，但不太清楚到底发生了什么。

带条件的单极三角波

三角波上升然后再次下降。 用浮点数表达是相当烦人的，但在整数领域这很容易：

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	if (phase & 0x80000000 == 0) {
		outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
	} else {
		outbuf[i] = (!phase) as i32;
	}
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// movpl out, phase  /* count-up in +phase */
// mvnmi out, phase  /* count-down in -phase */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1     -       -
     / \     / \
    /   \   /   \
   /     \ /     \
0 -       -       -



-1

注意 ！ 是补码运算符，意味着同相的所有位都会翻转。

该值通常从 0 上升到 0x7FFFFFFF。 一旦到达 0x80000000，我们就开始反转该数字。 !0x80000000 = 0x7FFFFFFF、!0x80000001 = 0x7FFFFFFE，依此类推，所有的倒数到 !0xFFFFFFFF = 0。

请注意，这意味着我们的三角形的尖端非常平坦，因为 0 出现了两次，0x7FFFFFFF 也是如此。 在处理 32 位相位时，您实际上永远不会听到这种声音，但对于较小的相位，这种声音可能会变得更加明显。

作为有符号整数，!x = (-x) - 1。 您可能想知道，为什么我们不使用实际的 - 运算符来取二进制补码，或者使用 .abs() 绝对值函数？ 看起来它会摆脱我们的扁平尖端！ 嗯，几乎可以，只是有一个大问题：INT_MIN.abs() == INT_MIN。 0x80000000 的二进制补码是 0x80000000。 当我们输出带符号的音频样本时，我们的波在尖端会有一个非常令人讨厌的环绕式负尖峰，如下所示：

1      |        |
      /|\      /|
     / | \    / |
    /  |  \  /  |
0  /   |   \/   |
       |        |
       |        |
       |        |
       |        |
-1     |        |

带条件的双极三角波

有时输出单极波可能很好，但通常您想要一个在负和正之间振荡的波。 我们的解决方案很粗糙。 我们只需从三角形中减去直流偏移，就得到了。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);
	if (phase & 0x80000000 == 0) {
		outbuf[i] = (phase as i32) - 0x40000000;
	} else {
		outbuf[i] = ((!phase) as i32) - 0x40000000;
	}
}

// Arm assembly
// loop:
// adds phase, step
// movpl out, phase
// mvnmi out, phase
// sub out, 0x40000000
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

1          

      -       -
     / \     / \
0   /   \   /   \
   /     \ /     \
  -       -       -

-1

这会产生一个三角波，该三角波不跨越整个 i32 范围，而是跨越 -0.5 到 +0.5。 在实践中这从来都不是问题，因为在生成振荡器后您总是会进行某种音量控制，并且您可以考虑信号链中某处的音量损失。

三角形的数学计算

条件实现很容易理解，但您可以用更少的指令以数学方式完成完全相同的事情。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// Will be all-zeroes if phase is positive or all-ones if phase is negative
	let mut out = (phase as i32) >> 31;

	// When phase is positive, out = phase
	// When phase is negative, out = !phase
	out = out ^ (phase as i32);

	// Remove DC-offset
	outbuf[i] = out - 0x40000000;
}

// arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// eor out, phase, phase, asr 31
// sub out, 0x40000000
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

脉搏波

25%, 12.5%, 6.25%, etc.

通过在方波生成步骤之前对高位进行与运算，我们可以进一步限制方波的占空比以获得脉冲波。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	let mut out = phase;

	// Comment out lines starting from the bottom
	// to achieve desired pulse width.
	out = out & (phase << 1); // Cut pulse width in half to 25%
	out = out & (phase << 2); // Cut again to 12.5%
	// out = out & (phase << 3); // Cut again to 6.25%

	let mut out = (out as i32) >> 31;

	// out = INT_MIN when phase  < 0x80000000
	// out = INT_MAX when phase >= 0x80000000
	out = out + (-0x80000000);

	outbuf[i] = out;
}

// arm assembly
// loop:
// add phase, step
// and out, phase, phase, lsl 1
// and out, out, phase, lsl 2
// asr out, 31           /* can be single step with rsb
// sub out, 0x80000000      if 0x80000000 is put in register */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
0              |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
               |   |            |   |
-1 ____________|   |____________|   |

16.6%, 25%, 33.3%, 40%

我们还可以通过用单极三角波调制器对方波载波进行相位调制来产生脉冲波。 通过改变三角形的幅度，我们可以选择一些以前的方法无法提供的脉冲宽度。

前一种方法延长了低边缘，而此方法延长了高边缘。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	let mut tri = phase as i32;
	tri = tri ^ (tri >> 31);

	// Choose your fighter
	//
	// 1/6 wave
	// tri = tri << 1
	//
	// 1/4 wave
	// tri = tri >> 0
	//
	// 1/3 wave
	// tri = tri >> 1
	//
	// 2/5 wave
	// tri = tri >> 2
	//
	// square wave (no modulation)
	// tri = 0;

	let square_phase = phase.wrapping_add(tri as u32);
	let mut square = (square_phase as i32) >> 31;
	square -= 0x80000000;
	
	outbuf[i] = square;
}

// mov dc_offset, 0x80000000;

// add phase, step
// eor out, phase, phase, asr 31  /* out = tri */
// add out, phase, out, lsl 1     /* out = sq phase, shift sets width */
// asr out, phase, 31    /* can be single step with rsb
// sub out, 0x80000000      if 0x80000000 is put in register */
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
// bne loop

       |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
0      |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |
       |           |    |           |

向三角形添加相位偏移会对脉冲产生更多有趣的影响，但这超出了本文的范围。

正弦波的抛物线近似

我在这里扩展了“基本”的限制，但它是基本波浪的近似值，所以我们这样做是为了好玩。

如果您有快速 32 位乘法指令，则可以通过在相位的前半部分取抛物线，然后在相位的后半部分取该抛物线的负值来获得正弦波的近似值。

这种近似对于物理或图形来说还不够准确，但对于音频来说，它会给你一个比三角波更接近纯音的声音，而不是像真正的正弦波那样纯净。

这个的用例非常小众。 在 GBA 上，我不这样做，因为它比仅访问 ROM 中的正弦查找表慢。 在 rp2040 上我不这样做，因为虽然 ROM 访问很慢，但有足够的 SRAM 来保存正弦表。 在高端系统上，有如此多的缓存，如果您追求性能而不是这样做，您可以将正弦表预加载到缓存中。

据我所知，使用此功能的唯一真正原因是，如果您正在进行一些大小编码并且没有空间用于查找表，或者您想要它的独特声音。

let mut phase: u32 = 0;
let step: u32 = (frequency_in_hz * 0x100000000u64 / sample_rate) as u32;
let mut outbuf: [i32; num_samples];

for i in 0..num_samples {
	phase = phase.wrapping_add(step);

	// Saw wave at 2x frequency
	let mut out: i32 = (phase << 1) as i32;

	// Saw -> Unipolar triangle (see Triangle section)
	// Range 0 - 0x7FFFFFFF
	let mut out = out ^ (out >> 31);

	// We are going to calculate X - X^2 to get a
	// parabolic arc from 0 to half-phase. This is
	// can also be thought of as
	// lerp(from: X, to: 0, alpha: X)
	let x = out;

	// Scale to 0 - 0x7FFF
	let mut out = phase >> 16;

	// Output arc from 0 - 0x4000FFFE
	out = out * out;
	out = x - out

	// Negate the second arc in the second half of
	// the phase
	out = out ^ ((phase as i32) >> 31);

	// Final output: -0x4000FFFF - 0x4000FFFE
	outbuf[i] = phase as i32;
}

// mul out, tmp       with single reg */
// sub out, x, out
// eor out, out, phase, asr 31
// str out, [outbuf], 4
// subs count, 1
